SOS 1.

Transitioning to AoE, communicating my core values.

My Product is a signature 12 week course aimed at women 40+ to reclaim and increase their physical strength and to feel more confident in themselves so they can be adventurous and age with grace.

 

It’s based around 3 pillars:

 

 

ACT 1 – Movement Snacks (how to bring physical activity into your everyday life in a fun and playful way)

 

ACT 2 – Positive Reconnection - to self and environment (Mindset around how you to talk to yourself and opportunities that you see in your environment – re-imagining who you are, identity and possibility.

 

ACT 3 – Positive Ageing (living in the moment and feeling good about the future and knowing that what you do now is going to put you in good stead for the next week, month, year and decade.

 

 

--------------------------

 

 

ACT 1: 1 The Teaser: set up: immediate, quick, grab attention, where is it? Day/night? Create empathy, everyday life, elicit emotional connection,

 

VALUE: The magic happens outside. Go outside. – where to play and move. Who gets to say how and where we move.

 

Subject line: The magic happens outside

 

 “Get down from there!”

 

I was too lazy and unmotivated to join a club, do sport again or buy any equipment.

And no. 

You wouldn’t find me in a gym.

I felt pretty average.

 

I didn’t look like ‘Mr. Blobby’ the soft mint cartoon character, nor was I a lean, mean fighting machine.

 

2004, 34 years old, I’d just moved from the south west of England to London via extended periods and projects in Southern California filming pool skaters (people who skateboard in empty swimming pools).

 

London was intellectually and culturally stimulating.

 

But a place to play, move and get stronger?

 

Nope. That wasn’t on my radar.

 

When you’re used to the right to roam around the English countryside, the dense urban terrain wasn’t a place for movement.

 

Or I thought it wasn’t.

 

My mind moved, raced, ducked and dived via exhibitions, night classes, art and history.  

It’s hard not to be stimulated in a city with all that.

 

And my body?

 

The black snot in my nose at the end of the day after travelling on the tube was a constant reminder of the environmental stressors and constraints.

 

There was a disconnect.

 

But I was interested.

 

I liked movement and movement people. The dancers, snowboarders, the athletes. 

The examples of what’s possible and inspiring. 

 

And they always looked really good! 

 

And of course the marginal creatives. My fellow square pegged humans in a world of round holes. 

 

People who moved in and around things in ways they weren’t designed for were artists!

Imagination and possibility wrapped up in urban cool.

 

Skateboarders were one thing, but this new no-boarding parkour was a whole new deal.

 

Movement can happen everywhere.

 

The minimalism, the lack of anything other than the body. Adapting to the environment. The bricks, the walls, the railings, edges and trees .

 

No one was pointing their toes, getting prizes or putting on a show.

 

Yet…

 

I wasn’t an athlete, I wasn’t planning a change of career, I was 20 years older than anyone who I’d seen do it. 

 

And I missed nature. 

 

And after all, who gets to say HOW and WHERE we move? What IS normal?

 

·      Everyone has a body therefore EVERYBODY can find a way of moving that suits them.

·      Spending time outside and connecting to your environment can have deeply psychological benefits

·      10 minutes of movement is enough to change how you feel and improve cognitive performance

·      A little everyday is better than extremes of all or nothing.

·      Movement heals and inspires. – depression etc.

·      Nobody is too busy to move

·      It’s normal to feel uninspired and too tired to move

·      When you’re too tired to move that’s when you need to move

 

 

‘you probably have no interest in walking on a rail or being the weirdo in the park and that’s cool, but the principles I learned and leveraged to be stronger, more brave, make friends with fear, and become a happy mover, ageing with confidence from my adventures in the parkour world can be applied to any activity. 

 

Did I choose a career change to embrace movement when I was busy being an independent filmmaker?

 

More on that tomorrow,

 

Cheers 

Julie

 

P.S. 

 

If you want a look at those early films, here’s what I made (link to Chlorine trailer)

 

 

--------------------------

 

ACT 1:2 The Want/need: what are they looking for, taken somewhere new, place or circumstances, they have to figure out what’s happening Hopeless to hopeful

 

VALUE: See it to Be it (lack of role models)

 

Subject line: Have you seen my hats? 

 

 You don’t look like someone who does parkour…

 

I don’t really know which one was my favourite, but on a Tuesday evening after Brownies I would run home to be on time to watch Charlies Angels.

 

They were fashionable, figure it out clever detectives, had no super powers other than being who they were. 

 

Where did they go?!

 

Non-super hero everyday Female action heroes were a scarecity by the time I started training.

 

Like most clever people I wasn’t doing what I knew I wanted to do, needed to do and should do to make me feel happier, stronger, healthier and more relaxed.

 

So clever…

 

I procrastinated for 6 months before taking my first parkour session.

 

It was hard for me to imagine me moving with the pack. The younger, stronger and infinately more capable athletes.

 

Where were my role models?

 

It’s easier to be it when you see it. 

 

Mainstream media rarely showed regular sporting female athletes, female lead characters, speaking roles, half of the adult population not being the experts on the news, the journalists – you get my point -  put in Susan Sarendon media statistics.

 

Representation matters. 

 

You know all the other middle aged slackers/ NOT the type A personalities who would be in the gym at 5am before work. 

 

I’m definitely more aligned with the generation X easy going chilled creatives and slackers. Filmmakers who were now trying to balance on railings and be guided to run up a wall. 

 

When it came to movement in midlife, very little spoke to me as it had when I was a teenager.

 

I had fallen out of love with movement without so much as a ‘it’s not you it’s me’ conversation.

 

And there I was 20 years after sitting down a lot realising that mainstream fitness was no place for me. 

 

And here was this activity with people who were stupidly silly strong, brave, confident, exploring, slightly mischievous and bringing the city to life for me.

 

It ticked a lot of boxes.

 

The sketchy housing estate I walked past on the way home turned out to be an amazing training spot. 

 

Guess what, no more fear when I walked home anymore. Instead checking the walls, imagining what I COULD do. 

 

My perception of the place had changed, 

 

And there it was.

 

Possibility. Perception and reality shifting. Orbiting with me in the middle. 

 

When you see a beginner doing parkour it can look a bit silly to the everyday passer-by. The hard focus to do something a young child would waltz up and do with ease and yet you can be frozen and fearing the consequences.

 

Feeling self-conscious.

 

Like many people, that was also part of the appeal. There was something of the weird and wonderful that spoke to me.

 

“I don’t know what I want but I want it now!” Epitomised by exploring the weird and wonderful of great British comics and films. 

·       'It wouldn't be a million miles wide of the mark to call "Sir Henry at Rawlinson End" a missing link between Monty Python and "Withnail & I", but as the brainchild of Vivian Stanshall - pack leader of the Bonzo Dog Doo-Dah Band - it has a place in the pantheon of sophisticated English silliness all of its own.' ***** – Time Out

 

 

 

When starting a new movement practice it’s normal and ok to feel EMOTIONAL!

 

·      Anxiety

·      uncomfortable

·      Not always make progress

·      Go slowly

·      Question everything

 

 

If you care about any of these things then what I’m about to share tomorrow will strike a chord.

 

It felt radical…

 

If you want to see those parkour spots – Latimer Road pk film

 

 

--------------------------

 

 

 

ACT 1:3 The Plan: brief, doesn’t always voice the plan just starts it, what steps needed to take to succeed in grasping their need. Moves to a goal. Outwardly visible finish line, formulate plan, obstacle gets bigger, first it’s playground then emotional layers revealed

 

 

VALUE: Barefoot strong, the world is a playground, starts to move more

 

Subject line: The world is a playground

 

We are born to run, crawl, jump, climb, throw, catch and much more!

 

Oh my god, can anyone see me?

 

Is anyone watching?

 

Just do it.

 

STOP following the lines.

 

Such a simple task yet so much resistance!

 

Reading the book Born to Run was a deeply moving experience for me on many levels. My uncle was in hospital with a broken neck and as my parents were on the other side of the world, I was by his side everyday. 

 

He also had dementia. It was complicated. 

 

My days in the hospital were long, heartbreaking and worrying. What were his future options and care plan? 

 

I had started running on the moors (Dartmoor National park) in the mornings and evenings to help with the stress, get fresh air, nature and movement. 

 

It helped.

 

When I felt tired I chose to run for him. Sometimes crying, other times smiling and inspired by him. He was the one in the family with more creative flair and who introduced me to illustration, photograhy and jazz.

 

Guided by my parkour teachers and the philosophy of the Yamakasi group, you never did sets of 10, it was always 11, 21, or 101 or 10001. You always had ONE MORE if you wanted. And you dedicated that one to someone else.

 

You don’t train just for you.

I dedicated parts of my running to my uncle. (And still do.)

 

Be strong to be useful.

 

In the quiet hospital hours I read. 

 

I read of the Tarahumara in the Copper canyons of Mexico, their kindness, struggles and way of living and of course their RUNNING.

 

And style of footwear. Their ability to feel the ground beneath them and inform the body of proper technique rather than having it on mute, perpetuating over-training and injuries. The heel strikers who fight the ground with every step…I shudder at the thought of every micro-trauma and future knee and hip ache and pain.

 

One of the parkour guys in London always trained and ran barefoot so I knew it was all possible and on my return to the city the book was doing the rounds.

 

Off I went to the vivobarefoot store and drank the minimalist shoe cool aid.

 

I also had a serendipitous encounter with my old Tai Chi teacher on Ladbroke Grove who introduced me to Rolfing (fascia manipulation) as he fixed 3 years of bi-lateral achilles tendonitis in a 30 minute sweaty palmed, Chinese burns type session that made me swear at him a LOT! Like good friends do…. 

 

There is no strong healthy body without happy, healthy strong feet!

 

There are no straight lines in nature but the field outside the sports centre had them and was a definite square shape with lines around the edge. 

 

To stop running in straight lines. To invite a wide vision. To move in more ways.

It was an act of micro-bravery and conviction. 

Not running in a straight line felt radical!

 

In different ways and directions.

 

To zig and zag.

 

Not everyone has the personality to go and start being wild and run ’outside of the box’. 

Running in a straight line seems straight forward and uncomplicated. 

 

It also felt liberating to not go straight, more fun, more interesting, silly and freeing.

It wasn’t like I was going full Monty Python silly walks, just a silly route?

 

And I was pain free and building up my foot strength after being shod and confined in Nike Airs with orthotics inside. 

 

What’s the next best thing to being barefoot strong? 

·      Importance of feet

·      Yth

·      Yh

·      thghghg

 

 

I felt free from order, direction, the herd, the invisible movement police telling me how and where to move! 

 

My goal is to move more in ways that feel good, authentic, at ease, are fun, bring me joy, and have a sprinkle of challenge to see who I am in those moments.

 

It’s good to not take yourself too seriously either – and sometimes when you meet the author of one of your favourite books…funny things happen…oh and his donkey too and an Armish scooter photoshoot. 

 

and PS I’ll never wear the 5 fingers, just too ugly…

 

 

--------------------------

 

 

ACT 1:4 The Obstacle: what obstacles are they facing to achieve their goal? physical? inner psychological?

 

VALUE: Mindset matters – Mind Body Everything’s connected.

 

Subject line: The 1question that haunted me 

 

BUT DID YOU TRY?

 

I wanted to train more and wasn’t.

 

I knew what I should be doing, and wasn’t.

 

I wanted to but…

 

The weekly parkour classes and sessions were great but they weren’t on MY timetable. 

I didn’t want to train in the evenings or on the other side of town.

 

I wanted to go to the park down the road.

 

But there were people in the park.

 

And they didn’t do the things that I wanted to do.

 

They walked and sat and looked at things.

 

Nothing wrong with that, I wanted more though.

 

I wanted to balance on the perfectly positioned low round railing around the flower beds and to practise standing jumps from the ground up to the big stoned wall.

 

I knew they weren’t there to look at me but…

 

It was easier to stick with what I was doing, surely that was enough?

 

But….I wanted more! 

 

Ever felt that even though you know what you want and need you still don’t do it?

 

3 letter words are tricky little things and important it turns out.

 

Everything before ‘but’ is bullshit, so said a famous somebody and I tend to agree. 

e.g. 

I really like you but….

I’d love to do that but…

I want to move but…

 

And ‘and’ is the ultimate game of add-on (if you don’t what that is in a movement context you’ll find out soon)

 

But the one I’ve realised is the most important one for me is ‘yet’.

 

Yet is a game changer.

 

I can’t do…x,y,z, - a pull up, handstand, kong vault, train on my own etc. ….. YET.

 

Everything changes with yet.

 

Yet brings possibility.

 

Future options.

 

The potential of you being different in the future from where you’re at now.

 

Today.

 

Yet requires patience and the long game.­

 

No fast wins or silver bullets. 

 

I hadn’t trained parkour on my own YET.

 

Until I did.

 

Terrifying at the start so I went early, to ease in and see if I could get in the ‘zone’.

 

The low round railing welcomed me with it’s standardised architecture, with few wobbly spots to come undone from and great form. 

 

 

Nothing I train falls into the category of ‘spectacular’, maybe just a little bit odd.

 

It’s not everyday that you see middleaged women playing in the playground – especially if you’re not wearing your sports uniform or are with a small child.

 

People think that going outside to train in a physical thing, and yes it is, however it is SO MUCH MORE A MINDSET! 

 

Our thoughts create feelings that drive emotions that create actions.

 

Or inaction.

 

If we change the thought of “I can’t do it’ to ‘I can’t do it YET’, different actions will occur.

 

Movement is emotional. Don’t let the muscles and tendons and tissue talk distract you.

 

You are a living breathing bio mental emotional physical being. 

 

Our thoughts and emotions can as easily lead us to avoidance and distraction strategies than action of the kind we want.

 

 

It was time to step up onto the rail.

 

The point of no return.

 

 

In that moment I realised the obstacle is not the way – Ryan Haliday- or it is, and I AM the obstacle.

 

 

 

--------------------------

 

 

 

ACT 2:5 Growth, how did the character do after the obstacle at the end of 4, positive or negative?

 

Subject line: It looked like nothing and felt like EVERYTHING!

 

VALUE: Play – result of playing in the park – had a skip and jump in my stride. Felt free and light as a feather in my spirit. 

 

You know that space where you’re so into what you’re doing that the world around you becomes irrelevant and disappears. Feels so good!!! (Flow state good vibes)

 

Phew, off to a good start.

 

I set myself the challenge of walking around the low rails 3 times in a row without coming off.

 

Three quarters of the way round on the first lap I was oblivious to the general public who were busy with their sitting, walking and chatting. 

 

There might have been an occasional side glance but who knows and the best thing is, who cares!!!

 

As xxxxxxx suggests in 

The Subtle Art of Not Giving a Fu*k – worry about things that are worry of your worry. The act of training alone is not of the same seriousness as other anxieties we can carry in life.

 

 

But here’s the thing.

 

The neurochemistry is real and if we worry and have that anxiety the body will feel like it’s being chased by a lion even when we’re in a lion free zone. 

 

Decades after the playful exploratory will of being a child is psychologically drilled out of us with “get down form there, walk nicely, sit nicely, learn to behave, that’s not appropriate, the list goes on, there’s something stifling and depressing about the general state of the population and its attitude to movement and play. 

 

I wanted to re-wild myself!

 

Being an adult surely didn’t mean cancelling curiosity?

 

According Dr. Stuart Brown nothing lights up the brain like play. 

 

And if you want to perform better cognitively you should move yet the institutions, teachers, bosses and our own inner demons tell us to sit there. 

 

Follow the rules.

Follow convention.

Or move in certain ways in certain places at certain times.

 

My only certainty was that that didn’t work and didn’t inspire.

 

My first solo parkour session in the park looked like nothing to an observer yet I conquered mountains and left Holland Park a different woman than the one who had entered a few hours earlier.

 

It WAS possible to be a functioning member of society and bend the movement rules. 

 

I grinned and felt a spring in my step. 

 

If I’d done it once I could do it again.  

 

 

I Felt stupid that it had taken me so long and also proud that I had done it. 

 

I had pressed the ‘fuck it’ switch of caring what others thought and felt or saw. 

And I’d lost myself in the act of doing it.­

What I was doing didn’t make sense to everyone. 

 

Nonsense maybe? 

 

 

--------------------------

 

ACT 2:6 Setback, true success, false victory, major setback

 

VALUE: Strong resting- go rogue - 



Subject line: I should have called it..

 

MAINSTREAM FITNESS & THINKING ISN’T WORKING,



Pride, ego, getting injured, doing too much to ‘prove it’ to who?

Injured by my pride, had no idea of scale, the long game and mindset that could push through and a body that is not of the same tempo. 



A big price to pay. Weeks of recovery and not training. 

Should have known better.

Mainstream says push through

Don’t rest.

Eat less exercise more- nervous system fried. Writing and living on sainsburys fresh pasta - nutritionally empty, late night icecream and binge watching @$. What would Jack Bauer do?!!!!



That countdown clock was my life! 

The deadline.

The sense of trying to appear like a successful adult



I wanted to stop being the rebel but here I was again faced with the mainstream not calling me. Making no sense. Natural Movement in isolated parts.

Mind and body reconnected – next up muscles /body and connection to the environment

An expanded vision of possibility – the parkour mirror, not the vision.

 

 

After running- tight calves (adrenalyn/stress)

Didn’t know how to scale

Nature important – wanted to run round Hyde Park felt self-conscious

 

Was committed to my research  - who am I? 

Thomas, did I try – existential crisis

 

  

The invisible benefits of …..over the next x days, 

Going to show you some of the most radical information on getting movement into your life that you’ll come across.

 

P.S. open the loop for the next email, or link, - here’s what I came up with, you can check out the rest here.

If you want an inside look



 

Safety in numbers, 

Rushed it, wanted to prove something to myself, others and be seen to do more than I could – paid the price, got injured from my pride- Kaz jumps, Moberley,patience, the long game,

 

Slow learning/comparisons

After running- tight calves (adrenalyn/stress)

Didn’t know how to scale

Nature important – wanted to run round Hyde Park felt self-conscious

 

Was committed to my research  - who am I? Thomas, did I try – existential crisis

 

 

 

 

The invisible benefits of …..over the next x days, 

Going to show you some of the most radical information on getting movement into your life that you’ll come across.

 

P.S. open the loop for the next email, or link, - here’s what I came up with, you can check out the rest here.

If you want an inside look



 

 

--------------------------

 

ACT 2:7 Regroup plot twist ahead,

 

VALUE: Strong resting- the art of recovery, sleep/vegetarian diet/

 

Subject line: But I’m not a caveman!

 

I couldn't relate to the thinking and logic around the food.

 

I'm a sophisticated modern woman who likes technology and travel!

 

Thailand, 2011. Each meal was delicious and I felt full after each one. 





The MovNat week long adventure had been a blast and due to an unfortunate incident with my camera going swimming, I ended up doing the retreat 3 times. 





We moved and learnt skills everyday. To run, jump, climb, throw, catch, swim, fight, defend and more. Natural movement in nature. Loved it!





Early start, big breakfast, rest, more movement, lunch, siesta then the last playtime of moves for the day.





You guessed it, we feasted again!





What if I told you eating more food and more frequently would get you to feel stronger, calmer and lean out?





Everyone was preaching the paleo diet - to eat in a way that our ancestors had.





I protested and resisted. 

If they'd have had access to pizza they would have loved it and never turned it turn!!! 



And enjoyed the icecream too!!! I really like ice-cream.





Then it came.





The phrase that changed it all.





"How about thinking about eating in a nutrient dense way?" said Paul in his quietly spoken tone.





It clicked. 





Now THAT made sense.





Sometimes we need a reframe or to hear it in a more nuanced way.





How did I know what worked for me if I didn't explore, experiment and experience?





For 6 weeks in Thailand I ate no grains, no gluten, protein & nutrient dense food.





I arrived back in London tanned, strong, rejuvenated (a 6 week Thai holiday should do that for anyone) and ready to write the final introduction and submit my doctoral dissertation.





Was it the holiday, the sunshine, the lifestyle, the movement that set me up for success?





I went back to my parkour training and something interesting happened.





I trained as I did pre-Thailand, ate and rested in the cold and wet of the UK winter.





Instead of the typical cycle of DOMS (delayed onset muscle soreness) starting at 4pm the next day folllowed by 24 hours of exhaustion, I felt fresh.





Yes some sessions still gave me DOMs but not so many.

Less aches, less stiffness, more power and more ease.





The next day I felt good, I mean really good! 





If you can't recover you can't train. 





You get stronger when you rest, hence the phrase STRONG RESTING.





And that's why it's an essential part of my 12 week course.





How you eat and sleep are the biggest determinates of your ability to reclaim, increase and sustain your strength and mobility - and yes of course you need to be moving.





Moving and training is the breaking down part, you know that right?





What I was doing worked for a good while, until it didn't...

I missed out on 1 important factor.

More on that next time.





Think nutrient dense, in however or whatever way you like to eat. 





Real foods.





Movement, fresh air and sleep.





Find your groove. Train hard, rest harder 

 

PS – link somewhere to my blog post about basing my training around my recovery?

 

 

--------------------------

 

ACT 2:8 Confrontation – it’ll work out

 

Subject line: ‘Is it time to press the Fu*k it switch?

 

Just me, loads of young guns and an ex-Navy Seal…great! Or 

 

The choice was staring me in the face.

 

To do, or not to do.

 

To be authentic and brave or somehow style it out, slip away and make a lot of excuses that might or might not sound reasonable.

 

Tears were slowly rolling down my face even before Amos had finished telling us what the ‘fear test’ challenge was.

 

It was too much.

 

No warm up, a cold start. Day 2 of the Apex Parkour coaching cert.

 

I felt overwhelmed before I had arrived at the Apex Movement Centre gym in San Diego that morning.

 

I’d spent the previous weekend in North Carolina with my favourite people (The Gangemi family) and had laughed a lot and had an amazing time. 

 

My concussion 6 months prior had brought on many new things, post- holiday blues was one of them and easy to feel overwhelmed. The rollercoaster of highs and lows. And the lows were L O W.

 

Driving could make me feel overwhelmed.

 

Two groups of people having conversations both in ear shot fried my brain.

 

Injuries that no one can see and that you don’t always feel like talking about…

 

Parkour is a culture of effort and sometimes showing up is the hardest thing you’ll be asked to do.

 

I’m not at home inside a gym, even a parkour gym as beautiful as this one. 

 

And I can count the times I’ve fallen into or played in a foam pit on one hand. It’s not my gig.

 

If we’d been outside and jumping into water, now we’re talking!

 

Everyone had to choose a height that they felt very uncomfortable with and fall backwards from it. 

 

I don’t like heights.

 

Or foam pits.

 

Everyone had their own thing going on. The gym owner was the only person close to my age and he’d gone through the BUDS Navy Seal training – a walk in the park for him. The other young guns chose their height and fell. Many of them were coaches who worked and trained at the gym. Familiarity was on their side.

 

They had my back and were routing for me 100%.

 

My stomach was clenched tight, chest breathing shallow and fast, tears still slowly rolling with no attempt to hide them. It would be futile to try. I sniffed and snuffled and wept.

 

I expressed my fears and said out loud that this was a time to be brave. To do the thing that I write on social media about. 

 

There was no pressure on me to do it. 

I decided that I wanted to.

I didn’t know how long it would take me and that was fine. 

 

It helps me to see it to be it – or see someone successfully do the thing I’m going to do before I make an attempt.

 

I asked Amos to do my chosen fall several times for me to see it and for him to survive. He obliged with empathy and support. Thank you Amos!!!

 

My imagination was convinced that this single act had the potential to break my neck and ruin my life. Even if it didn’t break my neck it could definitely ruin my life. 

 

Somehow…

 

That’s thoughts for you (remember that sequence – (thoughts, feelings, emotions – actions…).

 

Thoughts aren’t facts. 

 

The purpose of the drill was to know how fear manifested in your own body. I knew. It wasn’t the first time. My knees knock together too. 

 

I used my tried and trusted phrase borrowed from Johann Vigroux one of my parkour coaches in London.

 

Sometimes it’s time to press the ‘Fu*k it switch’. 

 

It was time.

 

“3,2,1…F that Sh*t!” 

 

But before I did that, I did something else. Something far more important. 

 

I trained a move that made everything else possible.

 

I closed my mouth. I focussed my eyes ahead of me. 

 

I started breathing. 

 

Diaphragmatically.

 

I started with box breathing – same number of counts for the in, hold, exhale and pause.

 

Then extended the exhale, so 2 seconds in, 4 seconds out.

 

The neural switch was set.

 

Then Johann popped into my mind.

 

Breathing is the skill that most people don’t train and the one they’ll benefit from more than any other.

 

We’re all EFFECTIVE breathers – or we’d be dead, and few are EFFICIENT.

 

Look around at your social group next time you’re out or when you see someone who looks really stressed out and overwhelmed.

 

I’ll put my money on it – 

 

Mouth breathers. 

 

They might not be gawping big wide mouth bull frog styled open, but they won’t be closed, in and out through the nose, gentle air that you can’t hear.

 

List benefits of nose breathing?

 

1.      

2.     Fgb

3.     Bgfx

4.     Xfb

5.     Fbx

6.     Gfhdfg

 

 

If you start your movement training with 1 move, choose breathing. 

 

Reading this email if you’ve made it this far, are your lips touching?

 

Here’s what I came up with – (link to youtube breathing video?)

 

 

It was time to start coaching.

 

Cheers x J

 

 

 

--------------------------

 

ACT 3:9 The Resolution – the silver lining, a revelation, no more obstacles, epiphany vs revelation, it’s about discovery, the aha moment

 

Subject line: It’s personal.

 

VALUE: Eat breakfast – know where you’re at, what you need. Men and women are different. Energy, balance.happy generalist

 

Remember that thing about the caveman diet?

 

Well there’s a couple of things I missed out that are important. 

 

Are you moving and training a lot?

Yet you still feel low a lot of the time and still don’t feel so good?



It's supposed to work right?

Movement is good so more movement is better?

If you keep exercising then eventually that'll fix things right?



However, movement on its own is not enough and that’s a hard pill to swallow. There is a very inconvenient truth…

 

Our training doesn’t exist in a vacuum, everything we do in our 24 hours effects the other hours of the day.

 

E.g. how we sleep is a product of how we spent our day.

Did you eat well and frequently?

Are you emotionally stressed? 

 

 

Did you get outside and let your eyes be exploed to natural light in the morning and evening thereby setting your circadian rhythm and natural body clock?

 

 

YOU CAN'T EXERCISE OUT A BAD DIET!

 

Nope, not me, not you, not anyone. Don’t be fooled by how someone looks either. Some of the unhealthiest people I know are lean and look like athletes but in terms of health they are lacking.

 

Here's the thing...



If every cell in your body doesn't get the nutritional content it needs on a consistent basis, you won’t feel good. Physically and Emotionally! You’ll start to break down physically.



Yes you will have anxiety.



You will have burn out.



You will feel stressed.



You will cry easily and a lot.



Ladies as you head into peri-menopause you are not going to have a good time. 



And it’s not normal or natural or ok to keep suffering.

 

More is not always better. Balance is better.



Injuries don’t come from nowhere -unless a piano falls on your head. A lot, not all, but a LOT of the time there is a nutritional component at the root cause.

 

The most common form of stress my clients don’t realise they have is nutritional stress. 

 

I’ll go down that rabbit hole at a later date I promise. For now trust me on this one.

 

You will probably blame your aches and pains, lack of energy and motivation on many other factors in your life and not food because you don’t eat "That badly" compared to a lot of people you know.

 

You think you’re pretty healthy.



While we are all bio-individual there are a lot of things that work for most people most of the time.



Eat real food. Make your own sauces. Eat breakfast within 1 hour of waking up and have a protein portion the size of your fist in every meal. Lots of veggies. 



And to the midlife women, if you like you tea and coffee, see how it feels to have them AFTER food, never on it's own or before. It's a blood sugar thing.



Small meals often. 



Fast when you sleep, for midlife women this is NOT the time of life when you need more stress. 



Have you ever considered that your food might be a major stress trigger? 

 

I used to start the day with 2 cups of tea (normali-tea, black tea with milk) because I wasn’t hungry then wait for 2 hours and then cook some eggs. 

 

Those 2 hours of work time were a buzz! Brain on fire.

Coritsol naturally high as it should be in the morning and then super-charged with caffeine and no food.

 

Blood sugar destabilised for the rets of the day…just great 

 

And guess what, I tended to be somewhat emotional in the morning. Not a good look.

 

Those cavemen and women may have fasted as they didn’t know when their next meal was coming from and they probably didn’t stay up late binging on Netflix either. 

 

While intermittent fasting is all the rage in the weight loss world, anyone who wakes up not hungry (unless they had a midnight feast) that’s a bio-marker of stress.



Therefore, what worked for our ancestors might not work for modern living for the over-caffinated and under-slept. 

 

What’s the one meal that’s non-negotiable? 

 

Breakfast.

 

Find balance.

Change with the changes.

 

Put some fuel in the tank.

 

You wouldn’t drive your car on empty and expect it to get from A to B without damaging it in some way either now or further down the line.

 

 

If you don’t believe me try it for 3 weeks, everyday. 

 

 

 

 

--------------------------

 

ACT 3:10 Resolution/closure Do they get their goal? Not the same person, open loops to find out more about – Movement/Parkour/ Mindset/ Positive Ageing/ Strong resting

VALUE: movement heals & movement = health = freedom – be a happy generalist

 

Subject line: Parkour was just the tip of the iceberg…

 

We didn’t evolve to exercise.

 

I was passionate and all in with the parkour world.

 

The mindset, overcoming obstacles, the imagination and more importantly, the parkour mirror that let me see who I was in the moment. 

 

Stripped naked of ego, title and sometimes even shoes, just me my barefoot capabilites and mindset, facing an obstacle that I know I’m capable of overcoming yet too scared to do.

 

Eventually I got bored of the version of me that was too shy to try. 

 

Those are the days of ‘breaking the jump’… 

 

There was something I kept hearing from other people like me who wanted to move.

 

Yet weren’t and felt like this somehow labelled them a failure in life.

 

They were off track and never stuck to any programme or course they did. 

They kept talking about exercise.

That they “needed” to exercise.

That they didn’t like exercise.

 

We evolved to move, not to specialise or exercise.

 

 You can be a mover in your own unique way, to wear all the different hats of e.g. filmmaker, academic, dog walker and parkour lady/movement coach! All over the age of 50 

 

 

AND there’s a mismatch going on!



Our brain & body evolved to move & be physically active then rest.



Evolution moves slowly while lifestyles change at rapid speed & our mind & body still needs activity to be in a good state of health & well being.



We’re no longer continually physically active in many ways on a daily basis.



Exercise (voluntary physical activity) requires more calories & makes us hungry. It’s not really a ‘natural’ thing to do.



Lifestyle & responsibilities mean a lot of people simply don’t have physical activity written into their everyday & the result of that is depression, anxiety, weight gain, inflammation, diabetes, obesity & all the other modern ills.



So what to do?!!!



Firstly understand that it’s actually quite normal to not feel drawn towards exercising & why it’s a hard new habit for many people.



Secondly, find ways to be physically active everyday that are ideally both fun & practical or just one.



Walk the dog a lot!

Gardening

Dance

Housework

Walking & exploring

Camping with little equipment 

Play games

& bring a friend along & make it social.

 

Be a happy generalist!



We can’t rewind time & undo our lifestyle so let’s get more physical activity to live well, feel better, do more & feel positive about how we age.



My adventures into the parkour world were opening doors to not only the possibility of a better braver me, movement begets more movement and my love affair was blooming.

 

I felt free and unrestricted in many different environments as the world was no longer about set routes of going form A to B.

 

I could always take the fast race line or the scenic route. Most of the time I take the normal pathway, but my heart body and soul KNOWS and FEELS the other options.

 

To draw the route mentally and choose the playgrounds on offer.

 

To no longer need the stale air of any indoor workout.

 

To find joy in the built environment if I wanted to move.

 

Don’t get me wrong, I’ll choose time in nature every day of the week given the choice. 

 

 

I had found reconnection, I no longer felt like a blob, was happy to adventure again, and now I knew about quads instead of thighs!

 

Our mind and body thrive on variety. Every environment has opportunities.

 

 

Barefoot happy+ lifting heavy shit….next story…

 

Don’t know how to end this and go into a soft bridge?

--------------------------
